context.pr_title: Sanitize NaN, Infinite, -Infinite causing error when saving as PostgreSQL JSON
context.pr_body: ## What type of PR is this? - [x] Bug Fix ## Description When running query that output NaN, Infinite, or -Infinite, redash executor crashes with the error "Out of range float values are not JSON compliant" like below. Redash does not output any rows when the error was occurred. ``` [2025-02-19 04:48:49,040][PID:593][ERROR][rq.worker] [Job 6cfa1d1c-2a32-48c6-8bf4-0cd8f8ba6f32]: exception raised while executing (redash.tasks.queries.execution.execute_query) Traceback (most recent call last): ... File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/rq/job.py", line 1280, in perform self._result = self._execute() File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/rq/job.py", line 1317, in _execute result = self.func(*self.args, **self.kwargs) File "/app/redash/tasks/queries/execution.py", line 309, in execute_query ).run() File "/app/redash/tasks/queries/execution.py", line 253, in run updated_query_ids = models.Query.update_latest_result(query_result) File "/app/redash/models/__init__.py", line 738, in update_latest_result for q in queries: File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/sqlalchemy/orm/query.py", line 3534, in __iter__ self.session._autoflush() ... sqlalchemy.exc.StatementError: (raised as a result of Query-invoked autoflush; consider using a session.no_autoflush block if this flush is occurring prematurely) (builtins.ValueError) Out of range float values are not JSON compliant [SQL: INSERT INTO query_results (org_id, data_source_id, query_hash, query, data, runtime, retrieved_at) VALUES (%(org_id)s, %(data_source_id)s, %(query_hash)s, %(query)s, %(data)s, %(runtime)s, %(retrieved_at)s) RETURNING query_results.id] ... ``` The error occurs because JSON(PostgreSQL JSON type) does not support NaN, Infinite, -Infinite. This PR fixes the issue by sanitize NaN, Infinite, -Infinite value to None. This is the save behavior that JavaScript JSON.stringify() does. We can reproduce the issue by running following query on BigQuery or PostgreSQL. BigQuery: ``` select 1.0, cast('NaN' as float64), cast('Infinity' as float64), cast('-Infinity' as float64) ``` PostgreSQL: ``` select 1.0::float, 'NaN'::float, 'Infinity'::float, '-Infinity'::float ``` The issue was introduced by #6685 which removes simplejson. Before #6685 , NaN / Inf was converted to null by simplejson with ignore_nan=True. This PR change behavior for NaN/Inf as same as before #6685 . ## How is this tested? - [x] Unit tests (pytest, jest) - [x] Manually Unit test was added. Also, we can confirm by running the query on the description. Before this PR: After this PR:

agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis. ## Project Context (MASCA Analysis) ## Project Analysis ### README

[![Documentation](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-redash.io/help-brightgreen.svg)](https://redash.io/help/) [![GitHub Build](https://github.com/getredash/redash/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/getredash/redash/actions) Redash is designed to enable anyone, regardless of the level of technical sophistication, to harness the power of data big and small. SQL users leverage Redash to explore, query, visualize, and share data from any data sources. Their work in turn enables anybody in their organization to use the data. Every day, millions of users at thousands of organizations around the world use Redash to develop insights and make data-driven decisions. Redash features: 1. **Browser-based**: Everything in your browser, with a shareable URL. 2. **Ease-of-use**: Become immediately productive with data without the need to master complex software. 3. **Query editor**: Quickly compose SQL and NoSQL queries with a schema browser and auto-complete. 4. **Visualization and dashboards**: Create [beautiful visualizations](https://redash.io/help/user-guide/visualizations/visualization-types) with drag and drop, and combine them into a single dashboard. 5. **Sharing**: Collaborate easily by sharing visualizations and their associated queries, enabling peer review of reports and queries. 6. **Schedule refreshes**: Automatically update your charts and dashboards at regular intervals you define. 7. **Alerts**: Define conditions and be alerted instantly when your data changes. 8. **REST API**: Everything that can be done in the UI is also available through REST API. 9. **Broad support for data sources**: Extensible data source API with native support for a long list of common databases and platforms. ## Getting Started * [Setting up Redash instance](https://redash.io/help/open-source/setup) (includes links to ready-made AWS/GCE images). * [Documentation](https://redash.io/help/). ## Supported Data Sources Redash supports more than 35 SQL and NoSQL [data sources](https://redash.io/help/data-sources/supported-data-sources). It can also be extended to support more. Below is a list of built-in sources: - Amazon Athena - Amazon CloudWatch / Insights - Amazon DynamoDB - Amazon Redshift - ArangoDB - Axibase Time Series Database - Apache Cassandra - ClickHouse - CockroachDB - Couchbase - CSV - Databricks - DB2 by IBM - Dgraph - Apache Drill - Apache Druid - e6data - Eccenca Corporate Memory - Elasticsearch - Exasol - Microsoft Excel - Firebolt - Databend - Google Analytics - Google BigQuery - Google Spreadsheets - Graphite - Greenplum - Apache Hive - Apache Impala - InfluxDB - InfluxDBv2 - IBM Netezza Performance Server - JIRA (JQL) - JSON - Apache Kylin - OmniSciDB (Formerly ## Your Task When you receive a PR title and body, you must: 1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve 2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions 3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified 4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR ## Available Tools - `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project - `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders ## Required Output You must produce a structured output with: - `pr_title`: PR title - `pr_body`: PR body - `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR - `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation - `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation - `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis ## Guidelines - Explore the code before drawing conclusions - Search for files with names relevant to the PR - Identify specific functions, not just generic files - Be precise with paths (relative to base_project) - For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods

### File Overview
This module defines API resources and helpers for executing queries, caching or streaming results, and exporting them in multiple formats. It centralizes the logic to run queries, fetch or enqueue results, and serialize them for JSON, CSV/TSV, and Excel formats.

### Architecture
- Core helpers (error handling, filename utilities) support the RESTful resources.
- run_query is the central orchestration point: it validates the data source, applies parameters, checks for a cached result, returns it if present, or enqueues a background job to compute and store the result.
- Serialization is delegated to serializers (serialize_query_result, serialize_job, and format-specific serializers) and json_dumps for JSON output.
- Resources map to API endpoints:
  - QueryResultListResource.post: execute a query (or fetch recent results) based on provided parameters.
  - QueryResultResource: handles both creating (posting) and fetching results in multiple formats (json, xlsx, csv, tsv); supports CORS, caching headers, and generated download filenames.
  - QueryResultDropdownResource and QueryDropdownsResource: provide parameterized dropdown values for queries.
  - JobResource: exposes status and cancellation for background jobs.
- Access control and permission checks are performed via Flask-Login and Redash’s permissions utilities.

### Functions & Classes
- `error_response` — returns a standardized error payload with a status and HTTP code. Importance: used throughout to inform clients of failures in a consistent shape.
- `error_messages` — dictionary of predefined error responses (e.g., unsafe parameters, missing permission, missing data source). Importance: centralizes user-facing error messages for reuse across resources.
- `run_query` — core orchestration function. 
  - Description: Applies parameters to the query, handles data source states (paused/blocked), enforces parameter validity, checks for cached results (max_age), records an execution event, and either returns a serialized cached result or enqueues a background job to compute the result.
  - Importance: The primary path determining whether results come from the DB (cache) or require asynchronous computation (DB write path). This function is the main bridge to the DB write path via enqueue_query when no cached result exists.
- `get_download_filename` — constructs a user-friendly filename for downloaded results using the query’s name (sanitized) and retrieved timestamp, with a file extension based on the requested type. Importance: Ensures consistent and meaningful file naming for all result exports.
- `content_disposition_filenames` — builds Content-Disposition header values to handle ASCII and non-ASCII filenames safely for attachments. Importance: Ensures proper client-side handling of downloaded files across locales.
- `QueryResultListResource` — API resource.
  - `post` — Executes a query or fetches recent results based on incoming JSON params (query, max_age, data_source_id, parameters, etc.).
  - Importance: Entry point for clients to request query execution or retrieve cached results; determines whether to hit the cache or enqueue a new compute job.
- `QueryResultDropdownResource` — API resource.
  - `get` — Returns dropdown values for a given query, after access checks.
  - Importance: Provides parameter-generation support for dynamic query UIs.
- `QueryDropdownsResource` — API resource.
  - `get` — Returns dropdown values for a related dropdown query, with permission checks on the related data source if needed.
  - Importance: Supports multi-query parameter dependencies in the UI.
- `QueryResultResource` — API resource with helper and formatters.
  - `add_cors_headers` — Adds CORS headers when an Origin header is present and allowed by settings.
  - `options` — Handles CORS preflight requests for different filetypes.
  - `post` — Executes a saved query with optional parameter values and max_age handling; supports auto-limit, and permission checks including unsafe parameter handling for API users or view-only scenarios.
  - `get` — Retrieves query results by either query_id or query_result_id in a chosen format (json, xlsx, csv, tsv). Handles:
    - Cache/miss semantics and retrieval of latest cached results
    - Access control to the underlying data source
    - Event recording for API users
    - Response construction per format
    - CORS and download headers (filename via `get_download_filename` and `content_disposition_filenames`)
  - `make_json_response` — Serializes a QueryResult to JSON using json_dumps and returns a Flask response with application/json content type.
  - `make_csv_response` — Serializes to CSV using `serialize_query_result_to_dsv` with comma delimiter.
  - `make_tsv_response` — Serializes to TSV using `serialize_query_result_to_dsv` with tab delimiter.
  - `make_excel_response` — Serializes to Excel via `serialize_query_result_to_xlsx`.
  - Importance: Central hub for retrieving and exporting results in multiple formats; handles caching headers, exact filenames, and content negotiation.
- `JobResource` — API resource.
  - `get` — Retrieves information about a running job and serializes it.
  - `delete` — Cancels a running job.
  - Importance: Provides visibility and control over background computation tasks that are part of the DB write path.

- `ONE_YEAR` — constant representing the max-age for caching responses (in seconds), used to set Cache-Control headers when a cached result is returned.
  - Importance: Controls long-lived caching behavior for repeated result retrieval.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- NaN/Inf sanitization: The file delegates JSON serialization semantics to json_dumps and the query result-to-dict conversion (QueryResult.to_dict()), not implementing sanitization inline. The agent should inspect json_dumps and QueryResult.to_dict implementations to confirm how NaN/Inf are handled prior to JSON emission.
- JSON dump path: The JSON output path is explicit in QueryResultResource.make_json_response, which uses json_dumps({"query_result": query_result.to_dict()}). This is where JSON serialization occurs for API consumers.
- DB write path: The path that writes results to the database after computation is invoked via enqueue_query from run_query. If no cached result exists, run_query enqueues a Job (enqueue_query) and returns a serialized job payload. The actual DB write of the computed result happens as part of the background job processing (not shown here) and is surfaced to clients via subsequent cached results. The JobResource endpoints also relate to monitoring/canceling those background tasks.

Overall, this file is the primary entry point for executing queries, serving or persisting results, and exporting them, while delegating actual data sanitization, long-term storage, and heavy lifting to other components (json_dumps, serializers, and background workers).

### File Overview
This file defines the ORM models and related helpers for Redash’s persistence layer. It includes data sources, queries, dashboards, alerts, notifications, events, API keys, and several utility functions used to manage scheduling, hashing, and latest-result propagation.

### Architecture
- Core ORM models are built with SQLAlchemy and mixed in with common behavior (BelongsToOrgMixin, TimestampMixin, ChangeTrackingMixin, etc.).
- There are primary domain entities: DataSource, DataSourceGroup, Query, QueryResult, Visualization, Dashboard, Widget, Alert, ApiKey, NotificationDestination, AlertSubscription, QuerySnippet, Event, Favorite, and related helpers.
- Cross-cutting concerns:
  - Scheduling and latest-result propagation via Query.update_latest_result and scheduled_queries_executions.
  - Hashing and auto-update hooks via event listeners (receive_before_insert_update) to keep query_hash up to date.
  - Serialization helpers for caching/JSON operations (to_dict methods, json_dumps/json_loads usage).
- Relationships: DataSource → Query → QueryResult; Dashboard/Widget/Visualization tied to Queries; Alerts and Subscriptions tied to Queries; NotificationDestinations for alert routing.
- Caching and schema handling that involves JSON/JSONB fields and Redis (get_schema / get_cached_schema).

### Functions & Classes
- ScheduledQueriesExecutions — class
  - refresh — reloads executed-at timestamps from Redis.
  - update — records the execution time for a given query_id.
  - get — returns the last execution timestamp for a given query_id (converted to datetime if present).
  - Relevance: supports scheduling/outdated-query logic by tracking when queries ran; used by outdated_queries and related scheduling.

- scheduled_queries_executions — instance
  - Global singleton used to access and mutate the scheduled executions state across the app.

- DataSource — class
  - __eq__ — equality based on id.
  - __hash__ — hash based on id.
  - to_dict — basic and extended serialization of a data source (with schema and options when requested).
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - create_with_group — creates a data source and attaches a default group.
  - all — returns data sources for an organization, optionally filtered by groups.
  - get_by_id — fetch by id.
  - delete — cleans up related queries/results and schema cache; notifies Redis.
  - get_cached_schema — fetches cached schema from Redis.
  - get_schema — fetches/ computes schema for the data source, with caching.
  - _sort_schema — sorts columns in a deterministic order.
  - _schema_key — Redis key for cached schema.
  - _pause_key — Redis key for pause status.
  - paused — Redis-based pause status check.
  - pause — mark as paused with optional reason.
  - resume — clear pause.
  - add_group / remove_group / update_group_permission — manage access groups.
  - uses_ssh_tunnel — whether SSH is configured.
  - query_runner — returns a configured query runner (with SSH if configured).
  - get_by_name — fetch by name.
  - groups — returns a mapping of group_id to view_only for this data source.
  - Relevance: central access point for data-source configuration, schema handling, and permission management.

- DataSourceGroup — class
  - Represents the association between a DataSource and a Group with view_only permission.
  - Fields: id, data_source_id, data_source, group_id, group, view_only.
  - Relevance: used for access control scoping of data sources.

- QueryResult — class
  - Fields: id, org_id, org, data_source_id, data_source, query_hash, query_text, data (JSONText), runtime, retrieved_at.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - to_dict — serializes result state.
  - unused — helper to fetch old results not linked to a Query.
  - get_latest — fetches the most recent result for a given data source and query hash, with optional max_age TTL logic.
  - store_result — factory to store a new result and persist it.
  - groups — proxy to the parent data source’s groups.
  - Relevance: stores JSON-formatted query results; used by Query.latest_query_data and dashboards/visualizations.

- should_schedule_next — function
  - Determines whether a query should schedule its next run based on previous iteration, interval, time, day_of_week, and failures.
  - Relevance: core logic used by Query outdated/scheduling computations.

- Query — class
  - Core representation of an executable user query.
  - Key fields: id, version, org_id, org, data_source_id, data_source, latest_query_data (relationship to QueryResult), name, description, query_text, query_hash, api_key, user_id, user, last_modified_by, last_modified_by_id, is_archived, is_draft, schedule, interval, schedule_failures, visualizations, options, search_vector, tags.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - archive — marks archived, clears schedule, and cleans up related visuals/alerts; can record changes if a user is provided.
  - regenerate_api_key — changes the API key.
  - create — creates a new query and a default TABLE visualization.
  - all_queries — returns queries visible to a set of groups (and optional drafting/archiving constraints).
  - favorites — returns queries favorited by a user.
  - all_tags — aggregates tags usage for a user.
  - by_user / by_api_key / past_scheduled_queries / outdated_queries — various helpers for filtering.
  - search / search_by_user / recent — search-related helpers.
  - get_by_id — fetch by id.
  - all_groups_for_query_ids — raw SQL helper to fetch group-permission data for a set of query ids.
  - update_latest_result — updates latest_query_data for all queries matching the same query_hash and data_source; returns affected query ids; central for keeping “latest” hooks in sync.
  - fork — creates a copy of the query and its visualizations for a new user.
  - runtime / retrieved_at — accessors for the latest_query_data metrics.
  - groups — proxies to the parent data_source’s groups.
  - lowercase_name — hybrid property for case-insensitive sorting; expression variant provided for SQL.
  - parameters / parameterized — access to parameterized query metadata.
  - dashboard_api_keys — fetches API keys associated with dashboards using this query.
  - update_query_hash — recomputes the query_hash potentially applying parameter substitutions; triggers the before_insert/before_update hooks to refresh the hash.
  - receive_before_insert_update — event handler wired to Query, updating the query hash before insert/update.
  - query_last_modified_by — ensures last_modified_by_id mirrors user_id on changes.
  Relevance: central representation of user-defined queries; update_latest_result is explicitly implemented here to propagate results to all matching queries.

- Favorite — class
  - is_favorite — checks if a given user has favorited an object.
  - are_favorites — given a list of objects, returns the IDs of those favorited by a user.
  - Relevance: user-specific favorites feature, used by queries/dashboards to filter/sort.

- OPERATORS — dict
  - Maps textual operators to Python comparison lambdas (e.g., ">", "<=", "==", etc.), plus backward-compatible aliases.
  - Relevance: used by alert evaluation and state determination.

- next_state — function
  - Computes the state transition for an alert given operator, value, and threshold.
  - Handles numeric vs boolean vs string values; returns UNKNOWN_STATE / TRIGGERED_STATE / OK_STATE.
  - Relevance: underlying logic for Alert.evaluate.

- Alert — class
  - UNKNOWN_STATE / OK_STATE / TRIGGERED_STATE / TEST_STATE constants.
  - Fields: id, name, query_id, query_rel, user_id, user, options, state, subscriptions, last_triggered_at, rearm.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - all — fetches alerts visible to a set of groups.
  - get_by_id_and_org — fetch by id within an org.
  - evaluate — evaluates conditions against latest_query_data to compute a state.
  - subscribers — returns users subscribed to this alert.
  - render_template / custom_body / custom_subject — render alert messages with Mustache templates.
  - groups / muted — accessors for grouping and mute state.
  Relevance: core alerting mechanism, including evaluation, templating, and subscription.

- generate_slug — function
  - Generates a unique slug for dashboards, ensuring no collision within the org.
  - Relevance: dashboard identification and URL routing.

- Dashboard — class
  - Fields: id, version, org_id, org, slug, name, user_id, user, layout, dashboard_filters_enabled, is_archived, is_draft, widgets, tags, options.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - name_as_slug / lowercase_name — convenience properties for sorting and slugging.
  - all / search / search_by_user — retrieval and search helpers.
  - all_tags — aggregates tags usage for an org/user.
  - favorites — user’s favorite dashboards.
  - by_user — dashboards owned by a user.
  - get_by_slug_and_org — lookup by slug within an org.
  - fork — copies a dashboard and its widgets.
  Relevance: user-facing collection of visualizations that hold queries/dashboards.

- Visualization — class
  - Fields: id, type, query_id, query_rel, name, description, options.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - get_by_id_and_org — fetch by id within an org.
  - copy — returns a shallow copy representation for recreation when forking dashboards/visualizations.
  Relevance: represents a visualization attached to a query, forming the UI.

- Widget — class
  - Fields: id, visualization_id, visualization, text, width, options, dashboard_id.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - get_by_id_and_org — fetch by id within an org.
  - copy — copies widget properties for dashboard/forking.
  Relevance: building blocks of dashboards; links to Visualizations and Dashboards.

- Event — class
  - Fields: id, org_id, org, user_id, user, action, object_type, object_id, additional_properties, created_at.
  - __str__ — string representation.
  - to_dict — serializes event details.
  - record — classmethod to create an Event from a dict-like payload.
  Relevance: activity/event logging.

- ApiKey — class
  - UNKNOWN fields: org_id, org, api_key, active, object_id, created_by_id, created_by.
  - __tablename__ = "api_keys"
  - get_by_api_key / get_by_object / create_for_object — accessors/creator helpers for API keys.
  Relevance: API key management for objects (queries, dashboards, etc.).

- NotificationDestination — class
  - Fields: id, org_id, org, user_id, user, name, type, options (encrypted), created_at.
  - __tablename__ = "notification_destinations"
  - to_dict — serializes destination with optional options (schema-masked).
  - destination — lazily obtains a concrete destination handler via get_destination.
  - all — list within an organization.
  - notify — delegates to destination, with options/schema applied.
  Relevance: external notification endpoints for alerts (Slack, email, etc.).

- AlertSubscription — class
  - Fields: id, user_id, user, destination_id, destination, alert_id, alert.
  - __tablename__ = "alert_subscriptions"
  - to_dict — serialization including destination details if present.
  - all — fetch subscriptions for an alert.
  - notify — triggers the destination (or falls back to email destination for user subscriptions).
  Relevance: manages who gets alerted and how.

- QuerySnippet — class
  - Fields: id, org_id, org, trigger, description, user_id, user, snippet.
  - __tablename__ = "query_snippets"
  - all — list snippets for an org.
  - to_dict — serialization.

### File Overview
This module defines the core abstractions and utilities for Redash query runners, including a registry for different runners, base classes (for SQL and HTTP-based sources), SQL parsing helpers, type guessing, and SSH-tunnel integration. It does not implement or apply JSON dumps sanitization or NaN/Inf handling for serialized results; instead, it provides the framework within which query results are produced and could be serialized elsewhere in the codebase.

### Architecture
- Global registry and registration:
  - query_runners: a dictionary holding registered query runner classes by type.
  - register: decorator to conditionally register a runner class.
  - get_query_runner: factory to instantiate a runner by type.
  - get_configuration_schema_for_query_runner_type: fetches the configuration schema for a given type.
  - import_query_runners: dynamically imports modules to register their runners.
- Core base classes:
  - BaseQueryRunner: foundational interface and utilities for all runners (SQL and HTTP). Handles configuration, basic query annotation, connection testing, column fetch helpers, and generic result handling.
  - BaseSQLQueryRunner(BaseQueryRunner): SQL-oriented features, including query limiting, SQL parsing helpers, and schema/statistics gathering placeholders.
  - BaseHTTPQueryRunner(BaseQueryRunner): HTTP-oriented runner with configuration schema and authenticated request support.
- Helpers and utilities:
  - split_sql_statements, combine_sql_statements: utilities to manage and normalize multi-statement SQL input.
  - find_last_keyword_idx: helper used to inspect SQL structure.
  - guess_type, guess_type_from_string: heuristics to infer column types from values or strings.
  - with_ssh_tunnel: decorator to transparently wrap a runner with an SSH tunnel when needed.
- SSH tunneling integration:
  - with_ssh_tunnel wraps run_query to transparently establish an SSH tunnel, swap host/port for the duration of the query, and restore them afterward.

### Functions & Classes
- split_sql_statements — Normalizes and splits a multi-statement SQL string by removing trailing comments and semicolons, and filtering out empty statements. Important for correctly applying limits to the last statement in a batch.
- combine_sql_statements — Joins multiple SQL statements with ";\n" as a single string. Used to reconstruct a sanitized query_text after per-statement processing.
- find_last_keyword_idx — Scans a parsed SQL tree for the index of the last keyword token, used to determine if a query has a trailing LIMIT/OFFSET or similar.
- InterruptException — Simple exception type used to signal interruptions (likely in async or long-running contexts).
- NotSupported — Simple exception type used to indicate unsupported features or operations.
- BaseQueryRunner — Primary abstract class for all query runners.
  - __init__ — stores configuration and sets syntax to "sql" by default.
  - name — class method returning the class name.
  - type — class method returning the lowercase class name.
  - enabled — class method defaulting to True, used by the registry.
  - host (property) — gets the endpoint host from configuration; to be overridden if needed.
  - host (setter) — sets the endpoint host in configuration; to be overridden if needed.
  - port (property) — gets the endpoint port from configuration; to be overridden if needed.
  - port (setter) — sets the endpoint port in configuration; to be overridden if needed.
  - configuration_schema — class method returning a configuration schema placeholder.
  - annotate_query — optionally prefixes the query with metadata annotations for debugging.
  - test_connection — runs a no-op query if provided and checks for errors.
  - run_query — abstract method, to be implemented by concrete runners.
  - fetch_columns — normalizes and deduplicates column names, returning a list of dicts with name, friendly_name, and type.
  - get_schema — must be overridden; default raises NotSupported.
  - _handle_run_query_error — logs and raises a standardized error if a run_error exists.
  - _run_query_internal — executes a query (via run_query) and returns the "rows" portion of results or raises on error.
  - to_dict — serializable representation of the runner's identity and configuration, used by client-side code; includes name, type, configuration_schema, and deprecated flag when applicable.
  - supports_auto_limit — property, default False.
  - apply_auto_limit — returns query_text unchanged; may be overridden.
  - gen_query_hash — computes a hash for the query text after applying an optional auto-limit.
- BaseSQLQueryRunner(BaseQueryRunner) — SQL-oriented extensions.
  - get_schema — builds and returns the database schema by delegating to _get_tables and _get_tables_stats when enabled.
  - _get_tables — placeholder that should populate the provided dictionary with table information.
  - _get_tables_stats — populates table size metadata by counting rows in each table via _run_query_internal.
  - supports_auto_limit — overrides to return True, enabling automatic limiting of SELECTs.
  - query_is_select_no_limit — inspects a parsed SQL to decide if a SELECT has no limit yet.
  - add_limit_to_query — injects a LIMIT clause into a SELECT query, respecting whether the limit should be placed after the SELECT or at the end.
  - apply_auto_limit — splits statements, applies limit to the last SELECT if needed, and recombines.
- BaseHTTPQueryRunner(BaseQueryRunner) — HTTP-oriented extensions.
  - should_annotate_query — overridden to False (no query annotation).
  - response_error, requires_authentication, requires_url, url_title, username_title, password_title — configuration flags and UI hints.
  - configuration_schema — builds the JSON schema for URL/username/password, including secret handling for password.
  - get_auth — returns HTTP basic auth credentials if provided; raises an error if authentication is required but not provided.
  - get_response — performs an HTTP request via a shared requests_session, handles authentication, and captures errors with structured messaging.
- query_runners — global registry mapping type to runner class.
- register(query_runner_class) — decorator to register a runner class if enabled; logs registration outcomes.
- get_query_runner(query_runner_type, configuration) — factory to instantiate and return a runner instance by type.
- get_configuration_schema_for_query_runner_type(query_runner_type) — returns the configuration schema for a given runner type, or None if not found.
- import_query_runners(query_runner_imports) — imports modules listed to register their runners.
- guess_type(value) — infers a column type from a Python value (booleans, integers, floats, or strings via string-based guessing).
- guess_type_from_string(string_value) — heuristic to detect types from string values, including integers, floats, booleans, datetimes, or defaulting to string.
- with_ssh_tunnel(query_runner, details) — decorator factory that, when applied, runs the target function inside an SSH tunnel context. It swaps the query runner's host/port to the tunnel’s local address for the duration of the call and restores them afterward. It also reassigns query_runner.run_query to include tunneling.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- This file is the central hub for query runner orchestration and type handling. While it does not implement NaN/Inf sanitization for JSON outputs, understanding its structure is essential for identifying where such sanitization would ideally occur or be wired in:
  - The result serialization path is not implemented here; _run_query_internal returns rows, and JSON serialization is handled elsewhere in the codebase.
  - TYPE_FLOAT, TYPE_INTEGER, TYPE_BOOLEAN, TYPE_STRING, TYPE_DATETIME, TYPE_DATE, and SUPPORTED_COLUMN_TYPES define how result columns are typed, which influences downstream JSON formatting but not the actual sanitization of NaN/Inf.
  - fetch_columns normalizes column names and preserves type metadata, which could impact how serialized results are shaped, but does not sanitize NaN/Inf values themselves.
  - The base and SQL/HTTP runner classes establish extension points where one could insert sanitization logic at the point of result generation or before serialization (e.g., in _run_query_internal, fetch_columns usage, or in a future to_dict-like serialization flow).
  - get_query_runner, register, import_query_runners, and guess_type(s) provide the extensibility and type-detection hooks you’d use to reason about adding a sanitization layer or a JSON-safe serialization step across different data sources.
- If the agent’s goal is to locate where NaN/Inf could be sanitized before JSON output, this file suggests that such sanitization is not implemented here and would need to be added either:
  - In the result handling path after _run_query_internal returns rows (before any JSON encoding in the consumer or API layer).
  - In a centralized serialization utility used by the UI/API that consumes the rows, possibly leveraging the column TYPE_FLOAT/type inference from fetch_columns.
  - As an extension point in BaseQueryRunner or BaseSQLQueryRunner (e.g., enhancing _run_query_internal to coerce NaN/Inf to null prior to returning rows).
- In short, this file defines the scaffolding and hooks where NaN/Inf sanitization could be introduced, but it currently does not implement such sanitization itself. The agent should look to the data serialization path that consumes the rows returned by run_query/_run_query_internal and/or the API layer that converts query results to JSON to implement robust NaN/Inf handling.

  - port (setter) — sets the endpoint port in configuration; to be overridden if needed.
  - configuration_schema — class method returning a configuration schema placeholder.
  - annotate_query — optionally prefixes the query with metadata annotations for debugging.
  - test_connection — runs a no-op query if provided and checks for errors.
  - run_query — abstract method, to be implemented by concrete runners.
  - fetch_columns — normalizes and deduplicates column names, returning a list of dicts with name, friendly_name, and type.
  - get_schema — must be overridden; default raises NotSupported.
  - _handle_run_query_error — logs and raises a standardized error if a run_error exists.
  - _run_query_internal — executes a query (via run_query) and returns the "rows" portion of results or raises on error.
  - to_dict — serializable representation of the runner's identity and configuration, used by client-side code; includes name, type, configuration_schema, and deprecated flag when applicable.
  - supports_auto_limit — property, default False.
  - apply_auto_limit — returns query_text unchanged; may be overridden.
  - gen_query_hash — computes a hash for the query text after applying an optional auto-limit.
- BaseSQLQueryRunner(BaseQueryRunner) — SQL-oriented extensions.
  - get_schema — builds and returns the database schema by delegating to _get_tables and _get_tables_stats when enabled.
  - _get_tables — placeholder that should populate the provided dictionary with table information.
  - _get_tables_stats — populates table size metadata by counting rows in each table via _run_query_internal.
  - supports_auto_limit — overrides to return True, enabling automatic limiting of SELECTs.
  - query_is_select_no_limit — inspects a parsed SQL to decide if a SELECT has no limit yet.
  - add_limit_to_query — injects a LIMIT clause into a SELECT query, respecting whether the limit should be placed after the SELECT or at the end.
  - apply_auto_limit — splits statements, applies limit to the last SELECT if needed, and recombines.
- BaseHTTPQueryRunner(BaseQueryRunner) — HTTP-oriented extensions.
  - should_annotate_query — overridden to False (no query annotation).
  - response_error, requires_authentication, requires_url, url_title, username_title, password_title — configuration flags and UI hints.
  - configuration_schema — builds the JSON schema for URL/username/password, including secret handling for password.
  - get_auth — returns HTTP basic auth credentials if provided; raises an error if authentication is required but not provided.
  - get_response — performs an HTTP request via a shared requests_session, handles authentication, and captures errors with structured messaging.
- query_runners — global registry mapping type to runner class.
- register(query_runner_class) — decorator to register a runner class if enabled; logs registration outcomes.
- get_query_runner(query_runner_type, configuration) — factory to instantiate and return a runner instance by type.
- get_configuration_schema_for_query_runner_type(query_runner_type) — returns the configuration schema for a given runner type, or None if not found.
- import_query_runners(query_runner_imports) — imports modules listed to register their runners.
- guess_type(value) — infers a column type from a Python value (booleans, integers, floats, or strings via string-based guessing).
- guess_type_from_string(string_value) — heuristic to detect types from string values, including integers, floats, booleans, datetimes, or defaulting to string.
- with_ssh_tunnel(query_runner, details) — decorator factory that, when applied, runs the target function inside an SSH tunnel context. It swaps the query runner's host/port to the tunnel’s local address for the duration of the call and restores them afterward. It also reassigns query_runner.run_query to include tunneling.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- This file is the central hub for query runner orchestration and type handling. While it does not implement NaN/Inf sanitization for JSON outputs, understanding its structure is essential for identifying where such sanitization would ideally occur or be wired in:

  - As an extension point in BaseQueryRunner or BaseSQLQueryRunner (e.g., enhancing _run_query_internal to coerce NaN/Inf to null prior to returning rows).

The agent should look to the data serialization path that consumes the rows returned by run_query/_run_query_internal and/or the API layer that converts query results to JSON to implement robust NaN/Inf handling.

import sqlparse
from flask import jsonify, request, url_for
from flask_login import login_required
from flask_restful import abort
from funcy import partial
from sqlalchemy.orm.exc import StaleDataError

from redash import models, settings
from redash.authentication.org_resolving import current_org
from redash.handlers.base import (
    BaseResource,
    filter_by_tags,
    get_object_or_404,
    org_scoped_rule,
    paginate,
    routes,
)
from redash.handlers.base import order_results as _order_results
from redash.handlers.query_results import run_query
from redash.models.parameterized_query import ParameterizedQuery
from redash.permissions import (
    can_modify,
    not_view_only,
    require_access,
    require_admin_or_owner,
    require_object_modify_permission,
    require_permission,
    view_only,
)
from redash.serializers import QuerySerializer
from redash.utils import collect_parameters_from_request

# Ordering map for relationships
order_map = {
    "name": "lowercase_name",
    "-name": "-lowercase_name",
    "created_at": "created_at",
    "-created_at": "-created_at",
    "schedule": "interval",
    "-schedule": "-interval",
    "runtime": "query_results-runtime",
    "-runtime": "-query_results-runtime",
    "executed_at": "query_results-retrieved_at",
    "-executed_at": "-query_results-retrieved_at",
    "created_by": "users-name",
    "-created_by": "-users-name",
}

order_results = partial(_order_results, default_order="-created_at", allowed_orders=order_map)


@routes.route(org_scoped_rule("/api/queries/format"), methods=["POST"])
@login_required
def format_sql_query(org_slug=None):
    """
    Formats an SQL query using the Python ``sqlparse`` formatter.

    :<json string query: The SQL text to format
    :>json string query: Formatted SQL text
    """
    arguments = request.get_json(force=True)
    query = arguments.get("query", "")

    return jsonify({"query": sqlparse.format(query, **settings.SQLPARSE_FORMAT_OPTIONS)})


class QuerySearchResource(BaseResource):
    @require_permission("view_query")
    def get(self):
        """
        Search query text, names, and descriptions.

        :qparam string q: Search term
        :qparam number include_drafts: Whether to include draft in results

        Responds with a list of :ref:`query <query-response-label>` objects.
        """
        term = request.args.get("q", "")
        if not term:
            return []

        include_drafts = request.args.get("include_drafts") is not None

        self.record_event({"action": "search", "object_type": "query", "term": term})

        # this redirects to the new query list API that is aware of search
        new_location = url_for(
            "queries",
            q=term,
            org_slug=current_org.slug,
            drafts="true" if include_drafts else "false",
        )
        return {}, 301, {"Location": new_location}


class QueryRecentResource(BaseResource):
    @require_permission("view_query")
    def get(self):
        """
        Retrieve up to 10 queries recently modified by the user.

        Responds with a list of :ref:`query <query-response-label>` objects.
        """

        results = models.Query.by_user(self.current_user).order_by(models.Query.updated_at.desc()).limit(10)
        return QuerySerializer(results, with_last_modified_by=False, with_user=False).serialize()


class BaseQueryListResource(BaseResource):
    def get_queries(self, search_term):
        if search_term:
            results = models.Query.search(
                search_term,
                self.current_user.group_ids,
                self.current_user.id,
                include_drafts=True,
                multi_byte_search=current_org.get_setting("multi_byte_search_enabled"),
            )
        else:
            results = models.Query.all_queries(self.current_user.group_ids, self.current_user.id, include_drafts=True)
        return filter_by_tags(results, models.Query.tags)

    @require_permission("view_query")
    def get(self):
        """
        Retrieve a list of queries.

        :qparam number page_size: Number of queries to return per page
        :qparam number page: Page number to retrieve
        :qparam number order: Name of column to order by
        :qparam number q: Full text search term

        Responds with an array of :ref:`query <query-response-label>` objects.
        """
        # See if we want to do full-text search or just regular queries
        search_term = request.args.get("q", "")

        queries = self.get_queries(search_term)

        results = filter_by_tags(queries, models.Query.tags)

        # order results according to passed order parameter,
        # special-casing search queries where the database
        # provides an order by search rank
        ordered_results = order_results(results, fallback=not bool(search_term))

        page = request.args.get("page", 1, type=int)
        page_size = request.args.get("page_size", 25, type=int)

        response = paginate(
            ordered_results,
            page=page,
            page_size=page_size,
            serializer=QuerySerializer,
            with_stats=True,
            with_last_modified_by=False,
        )

        if search_term:
            self.record_event({"action": "search", "object_type": "query", "term": search_term})
        else:
            self.record_event({"action": "list", "object_type": "query"})

        return response


def require_access_to_dropdown_queries(user, query_def):
    parameters = query_def.get("options", {}).get("parameters", [])
    dropdown_query_ids = set([str(p["queryId"]) for p in parameters if p["type"] == "query"])

    if dropdown_query_ids:
        groups = models.Query.all_groups_for_query_ids(dropdown_query_ids)

        if len(groups) < len(dropdown_query_ids):
            abort(
                400,
                message="You are trying to associate a dropdown query that does not have a matching group. "
                "Please verify the dropdown query id you are trying to associate with this query.",
            )

        require_access(dict(groups), user, view_only)


class QueryListResource(BaseQueryListResource):
    @require_permission("create_query")
    def post(self):
        """
        Create a new query.

        :<json number data_source_id: The ID of the data source this query will run on
        :<json string query: Query text
        :<json string name:
        :<json string description:
        :<json string schedule: Schedule interval, in seconds, for repeated execution of this query
        :<json object options: Query options

        .. _query-response-label:

        :>json number id: Query ID
        :>json number latest_query_data_id: ID for latest output data from this query
        :>json string name:
        :>json string description:
        :>json string query: Query text
        :>json string query_hash: Hash of query text
        :>json string schedule: Schedule interval, in seconds, for repeated execution of this query
        :>json string api_key: Key for public access to this query's results.
        :>json boolean is_archived: Whether this query is displayed in indexes and search results or not.
        :>json boolean is_draft: Whether this query is a draft or not
        :>json string updated_at: Time of last modification, in ISO format
        :>json string created_at: Time of creation, in ISO format
        :>json number data_source_id: ID of the data source this query will run on
        :>json object options: Query options
        :>json number version: Revision version (for update conflict avoidance)
        :>json number user_id: ID of query creator
        :>json number last_modified_by_id: ID of user who last modified this query
        :>json string retrieved_at: Time when query results were last retrieved, in ISO format (may be null)
        :>json number runtime: Runtime of last query execution, in seconds (may be null)
        """
        query_def = request.get_json(force=True)
        data_source = models.DataSource.get_by_id_and_org(query_def.pop("data_source_id"), self.current_org)
        require_access(data_source, self.current_user, not_view_only)
        require_access_to_dropdown_queries(self.current_user, query_def)

        for field in [
            "id",
            "created_at",
            "api_key",
            "visualizations",
            "latest_query_data",
            "last_modified_by",
        ]:
            query_def.pop(field, None)

        query_def["query_text"] = query_def.pop("query")
        query_def["user"] = self.current_user
        query_def["data_source"] = data_source
        query_def["org"] = self.current_org
        query_def["is_draft"] = True
        query = models.Query.create(**query_def)
        models.db.session.add(query)
        models.db.session.commit()

        self.record_event({"action": "create", "object_id": query.id, "object_type": "query"})

        return QuerySerializer(query, with_visualizations=True).serialize()


class QueryArchiveResource(BaseQueryListResource):
    def get_queries(self, search_term):
        if search_term:
            return models.Query.search(
                search_term,
                self.current_user.group_ids,
                self.current_user.id,
                include_drafts=False,
                include_archived=True,
                multi_byte_search=current_org.get_setting("multi_byte_search_enabled"),
            )
        else:
            return models.Query.all_queries(
                self.current_user.group_ids,
                self.current_user.id,
                include_drafts=False,
                include_archived=True,
            )


class MyQueriesResource(BaseResource):
    @require_permission("view_query")
    def get(self):
        """
        Retrieve a list of queries created by the current user.

        :qparam number page_size: Number of queries to return per page
        :qparam number page: Page number to retrieve
        :qparam number order: Name of column to order by
        :qparam number search: Full text search term

        Responds with an array of :ref:`query <query-response-label>` objects.
        """
        search_term = request.args.get("q", "")
        if search_term:
            results = models.Query.search_by_user(
                search_term,
                self.current_user,
                multi_byte_search=current_org.get_setting("multi_byte_search_enabled"),
            )
        else:
            results = models.Query.by_user(self.current_user)

        results = filter_by_tags(results, models.Query.tags)

        # order results according to passed order parameter,
        # special-casing search queries where the database
        # provides an order by search rank
        ordered_results = order_results(results, fallback=not bool(search_term))

        page = request.args.get("page", 1, type=int)
        page_size = request.args.get("page_size", 25, type=int)
        return paginate(
            ordered_results,
            page,
            page_size,
            QuerySerializer,
            with_stats=True,
            with_last_modified_by=False,
        )


class QueryResource(BaseResource):
    @require_permission("edit_query")
    def post(self, query_id):
        """
        Modify a query.

        :param query_id: ID of query to update
        :<json number data_source_id: The ID of the data source this query will run on
        :<json string query: Query text
        :<json string name:
        :<json string description:
        :<json string schedule: Schedule interval, in seconds, for repeated execution of this query
        :<json object options: Query options

        Responds with the updated :ref:`query <query-response-label>` object.
        """
        query = get_object_or_404(models.Query.get_by_id_and_org, query_id, self.current_org)
        query_def = request.get_json(force=True)

        require_object_modify_permission(query, self.current_user)
        require_access_to_dropdown_queries(self.current_user, query_def)

        for field in [
            "id",
            "created_at",
            "api_key",
            "visualizations",
            "latest_query_data",
            "user",
            "last_modified_by",
            "org",
        ]:
            query_def.pop(field, None)

        if "query" in query_def:
            query_def["query_text"] = query_def.pop("query")

        if "tags" in query_def:
            query_def["tags"] = [tag for tag in query_def["tags"] if tag]

        if "data_source_id" in query_def:
            data_source = models.DataSource.get_by_id_and_org(query_def["data_source_id"], self.current_org)
            require_access(data_source, self.current_user, not_view_only)

        query_def["last_modified_by"] = self.current_user
        query_def["changed_by"] = self.current_user
        # SQLAlchemy handles the case where a concurrent transaction beats us
        # to the update. But we still have to make sure that we're not starting
        # out behind.
        if "version" in query_def and query_def["version"] != query.version:
            abort(409)

        try:
            self.update_model(query, query_def)
            models.db.session.commit()
        except StaleDataError:
            abort(409)

        return QuerySerializer(query, with_visualizations=True).serialize()

    @require_permission("view_query")
    def get(self, query_id):
        """
        Retrieve a query.

        :param query_id: ID of query to fetch

        Responds with the :ref:`query <query-response-label>` contents.
        """
        q = get_object_or_404(models.Query.get_by_id_and_org, query_id, self.current_org)
        require_access(q, self.current_user, view_only)

        result = QuerySerializer(q, with_visualizations=True).serialize()
        result["can_edit"] = can_modify(q, self.current_user)

        self.record_event({"action": "view", "object_id": query_id, "object_type": "query"})

        return result

    # TODO: move to resource of its own? (POST /queries/{id}/archive)
    def delete(self, query_id):
        """
        Archives a query.

        :param query_id: ID of query to archive
        """
        query = get_object_or_404(models.Query.get_by_id_and_org, query_id, self.current_org)
        require_admin_or_owner(query.user_id)
        query.archive(self.current_user)
        models.db.session.commit()


class QueryRegenerateApiKeyResource(BaseResource):
    @require_permission("edit_query")
    def post(self, query_id):
        query = get_object_or_404(models.Query.get_by_id_and_org, query_id, self.current_org)
        require_admin_or_owner(query.user_id)
        query.regenerate_api_key()
        models.db.session.commit()

        self.record_event(
            {
                "action": "regnerate_api_key",
                "object_id": query_id,
                "object_type": "query",
            }
        )

        result = QuerySerializer(query).serialize()
        return result


class QueryForkResource(BaseResource):
    @require_permission("edit_query")
    def post(self, query_id):
        """
        Creates a new query, copying the query text from an existing one.

        :param query_id: ID of query to fork

        Responds with created :ref:`query <query-response-label>` object.
        """
        query = get_object_or_404(models.Query.get_by_id_and_org, query_id, self.current_org)
        require_access(query.data_source, self.current_user, not_view_only)
        forked_query = query.fork(self.current_user)
        models.db.session.commit()

        self.record_event({"action": "fork", "object_id": query_id, "object_type": "query"})

        return QuerySerializer(forked_query, with_visualizations=True).serialize()


class QueryRefreshResource(BaseResource):
    def post(self, query_id):
        """
        Execute a query, updating the query object with the results.

        :param query_id: ID of query to execute

        Responds with query task details.
        """
        # TODO: this should actually check for permissions, but because currently you can only
        # get here either with a user API key or a query one, we can just check whether it's
        # an api key (meaning this is a query API key, which only grants read access).
        if self.current_user.is_api_user():
            abort(403, message="Please use a user API key.")

        query = get_object_or_404(models.Query.get_by_id_and_org, query_id, self.current_org)
        require_access(query, self.current_user, not_view_only)

        parameter_values = collect_parameters_from_request(request.args)
        parameterized_query = ParameterizedQuery(query.query_text, org=self.current_org)
        should_apply_auto_limit = query.options.get("apply_auto_limit", False)
        return run_query(parameterized_query, parameter_values, query.data_source, query.id, should_apply_auto_limit)


class QueryTagsResource(BaseResource):
    def get(self):
        """
        Returns all query tags including those for drafts.
        """
        tags = models.Query.all_tags(self.current_user, include_drafts=True)
        return {"tags": [{"name": name, "count": count} for name, count in tags]}


class QueryFavoriteListResource(BaseResource):
    def get(self):
        search_term = request.args.get("q")

        if search_term:
            base_query = models.Query.search(
                search_term,
                self.current_user.group_ids,
                include_drafts=True,
                limit=None,
                multi_byte_search=current_org.get_setting("multi_byte_search_enabled"),
            )
            favorites = models.Query.favorites(self.current_user, base_query=base_query)
        else:
            favorites = models.Query.favorites(self.current_user)

        favorites = filter_by_tags(favorites, models.Query.tags)

        # order results according to passed order parameter,
        # special-casing search queries where the database
        # provides an order by search rank
        ordered_favorites = order_results(favorites, fallback=not bool(search_term))

        page = request.args.get("page", 1, type=int)
        page_size = request.args.get("page_size", 25, type=int)
        response = paginate(
            ordered_favorites,
            page,
            page_size,
            QuerySerializer,
            with_stats=True,
            with_last_modified_by=False,
        )

        self.record_event(
            {
                "action": "load_favorites",
                "object_type": "query",
                "params": {
                    "q": search_term,
                    "tags": request.args.getlist("tags"),
                    "page": page,
                },
            }
        )

        return response

### File Overview
This module defines the API endpoints and resource classes for querying, listing, creating, updating, forking, refreshing, and archiving queries. It leverages QuerySerializer to produce JSON responses and routes related to formatting SQL queries.

### Architecture
- The file exposes Flask routes and a set of class-based resources (subclasses of BaseResource) that handle various HTTP methods (get, post, delete) for queries.
- It integrates with models (Query, DataSource, etc.), authentication (current_org, current_user), permissions, and a shared serialization layer (QuerySerializer) to produce JSON API responses.
- There are helpers for search, pagination, and tagging, and a SQL formatting endpoint that uses sqlparse.
- The API results are serialized through QuerySerializer in many handlers, which is the key path where JSON is created and where NaN/Inf sanitization would be applied if implemented in the serializer layer.

### Functions & Classes
- `order_map` — Ordering map for relationships. (Module-level constant used by ordering logic.)
- `order_results` — Partial function binding default order and allowed orders. (Module-level constant used to sort results in various endpoints.)

- `format_sql_query` — description: Formats an SQL query using sqlparse and returns the formatted string in JSON. Importance: Exposes a utility endpoint for clients to format SQL before submission.

- `QuerySearchResource` — description: Resource handling search for queries. Importance: Part of the query search flow; redirects to the newer list API with search term for consistency.

  - `QuerySearchResource.get` — description: Handles search requests; redirects with a 301 to the updated list API including the search term. Importance: Bridges legacy search to the modern API path.

- `QueryRecentResource` — description: Retrieves up to 10 recently modified queries for the current user. Importance: Provides a quick-access list of user-specific recent queries.

  - `QueryRecentResource.get` — description: Queries models.Query.by_user and orders by updated_at desc; returns serialized results. Importance: Demonstrates how results are serialized for API consumers via QuerySerializer.

- `BaseQueryListResource` — description: Shared base for listing queries; provides common query retrieval logic and pagination support. Importance: Centralizes logic used by multiple list endpoints.

  - `BaseQueryListResource.get_queries` — description: Fetches either a search result set or all queries for the current user, with drafts included. Importance: Core data retrieval method used by concrete list resources.

  - `BaseQueryListResource.get` — description: Lists queries (supports q, page, page_size, and sorting); uses QuerySerializer for serialization; tracks events. Importance: Core listing endpoint illustrating how results are serialized and paginated.

- `require_access_to_dropdown_queries` — description: Validates dropdown query dependencies in a query definition; ensures groups exist and user has access. Importance: Enforces integrity and permissions when creating/updating queries.

- `QueryListResource` — description: Create a new query. Importance: Handles the creation workflow; prepares and cleans the payload, ties the new query to the user/org, and returns the serialized query.

  - `QueryListResource.post` — description: Processes incoming JSON for a new query, validates data source, checks dropdown dependencies, maps fields, marks as draft, persists, and returns serialized query with visualizations. Importance: Central to creating new queries via API; post-path is where a lot of object construction happens.

- `QueryArchiveResource` — description: List queries for the archive view (including archived and non-draft items as appropriate). Importance: Provides an alternate listing view for archived/filtered queries.
